claim: A same-repo reusable workflow (workflow_call) can use a local composite action via `uses: ./.github/actions/foo` after actions/checkout, resolving against the CALLER repo.

votes.lens: Official GitHub Actions documentation and changelogs only
votes.evidence:
Three official-docs facts combine to settle this for the SAME-repo case (I could not find one single sentence naming "caller repo" explicitly, so this is a direct combination of documented mechanics, not speculation):

1. General local-action resolution rule (docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax, section `jobs.<job_id>.steps[*].uses`):
   "You must check out your repository before using the action." ... "The path is relative (./) to the default working directory (github.workspace, $GITHUB_WORKSPACE). If the action checks out the repository to a location different than the workflow, the relative path used for local actions must be updated."
   → i.e. `./` always resolves against whatever repo the job's checkout populated into $GITHUB_WORKSPACE — it is not tied to where the *workflow file* physically lives.

2. The SAME page's own worked example for `on.workflow_call.secrets` shows this exact pattern used INSIDE a `workflow_call`-triggered job:
   ```
   pass-secret-to-action:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     steps:
       - name: Pass the received secret to an action
         uses: ./.github/actions/my-action
         with:
           token: ${{ secrets.access-token }}
   ```
   This is GitHub's own canonical example of a reusable-workflow job invoking a local composite action via `./.github/actions/...`.

3. Same-repo reusable-workflow reference syntax (docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/reusing-workflow-configurations, and mirrored in .../how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows): "./.github/workflows/{filename} for reusable workflows in the same repository. ... If you use the second syntax option (without {owner}/{repo} and @{ref}) the called workflow is from the same commit as the caller workflow."
   → Same-repo `workflow_call` jobs run at the same commit as the caller; a job-level `actions/checkout` with no `repository:`/`ref:` input therefore checks out that same commit of that same repository — which is by definition the repository containing `.github/actions/foo`.

Putting 1–3 together: for a same-repo reusable workflow, "caller repo," "repo containing the reusable workflow," and "repo containing the local action" are all the same repository at the same SHA, so `uses: ./.github/actions/foo` after a plain `actions/checkout` resolves correctly — exactly as GitHub's own example (#2) demonstrates. (I also found a GitHub Actions team member's 2024 community-discussion comment describing an in-progress `$/` syntax proposal to fix local-action resolution for CROSS-repo reusable workflows — that pain point is real but is a different scenario than the one in this claim, and a community discussion is not "official docs," so I'm not using it as the basis for the verdict, only as color confirming the same-repo case is the trivial/non-problematic one.)
votes.implication: This removes "can a reusable workflow even use our local composite action" as a discriminator between A1 and A2 — GitHub's own docs example shows the exact pattern (workflow_call job → checkout → uses: ./.github/actions/foo) working when everything is same-repo. So if you go with A2, you can still keep the actual build logic factored into .github/actions/kernel-leg/ and have the reusable workflow's matrix job just checkout + call it — you don't have to inline the whole build as bare steps in the reusable workflow. Decide A1 vs A2 on the other axes instead: A2 gives you a distinct job name per call site plus its own `permissions:`/`secrets:` boundary and is the natural fit if you want the matrix to literally be reusable-workflow-level (each leg a full called workflow, easier to point branch-protection's required check at a single aggregation job that `needs:` the workflow_call job); A1 keeps everything as one job's steps (simpler artifact upload/consume within the same job context, no cross-job artifact hop needed for the immediately-following job) and is less code for a single-job matrix. Note the one real caveat: if you ever want this reusable workflow callable from a DIFFERENT repo (not your case today, single-repo), local-action resolution becomes the documented-gap/footgun scenario from the community discussion — re-verify before doing that.

votes.evidence: Multiple independent sources agree the same-repo case works: (1) GitHub Docs "Reusing workflows" states that when a reusable workflow is called via the local/relative syntax (`./.github/workflows/{file}`, no `{owner}/{repo}@{ref}`), "the called workflow is from the same commit as the caller workflow" — i.e. no repo/ref mismatch is possible in the same-repo case. (2) GitHub staff (Yanjingzhu) in community discussion #26245 explicitly confirms the working pattern for a local action in the same repo as the workflow: "we need to add actions/checkout before running your local action... `uses: ./.github/actions/automatic-tests`" (no `@ref` suffix — appending one breaks resolution, which the claim correctly avoids). (3) Community discussion #18601 confirms the mechanism is real (checkout populates $GITHUB_WORKSPACE, then `uses: ./...` resolves against whatever was checked out) and shows it working once the correct repo is checked out. (4) The well-documented FAILURE mode — "runner searches in the caller's repository, not the reusable workflow's repository" (discussion #66094, maintainer Josh Johanning) and "resolve relative to the calling workflow's repository, not the reusable workflow's repository" — is exclusively a CROSS-repo problem (caller repo ≠ repo hosting the reusable workflow/action). Since the claim is explicitly scoped to a same-repo reusable workflow, caller repo and workflow-hosting repo are identical by construction, so this well-known trap does not apply.
votes.implication: Confirms A1 (composite action) and A2 (same-repo reusable workflow) are equally viable on pure mechanics: a same-repo workflow_call job can `checkout` then `uses: ./.github/actions/kernel-leg` without the cross-repo path-resolution trap. Two caveats matter for the actual A1-vs-A2 decision though. First, local `./` references (to either a composite action or a same-repo reusable workflow) structurally cannot carry an `@<sha>` suffix — GitHub resolves them to "the same commit as the caller" implicitly, so any SHA-pinning lint/policy the repo enforces on every `uses:` line needs an explicit carve-out for local paths (both A1 and A2 share this, so it's not a differentiator). Second, and this does differentiate: A2's workflow_call job, especially under a matrix strategy, surfaces in the Checks UI with GitHub-generated compound names (call-job-id / called-job-name (matrix-values)), which is more brittle to pin as the single required branch-protection check than A1, where the matrix'd kernel-leg job stays a plain job inside the caller workflow with a stable name. That pushes toward A1 for the "specific aggregation job as required check" requirement even though the local-action resolution question itself is a non-issue for A2.
votes.caveats: This CONFIRMED verdict is tightly scoped to same-repo usage exactly as the claim states. It does NOT extend to: (a) a reusable workflow stored in a different repo than the caller (well-documented, universally-reported failure — local paths resolve against the caller, not the workflow's own repo); (b) appending an `@ref`/`@sha` to the local action path (breaks resolution — GitHub treats it as a literal directory name); (c) a job in the reusable workflow that reuses the composite action WITHOUT its own actions/checkout step (each job is a fresh runner/workspace, so checkout is required per-job, which the claim already correctly stipulates); (d) checkout to a non-default path (e.g. `actions/checkout` with `path:`), which would require adjusting the local `uses:` path accordingly.

votes.evidence: GitHub's own community forum has multiple recurring threads about exactly this mechanism (Discussions #18601 'Accessing Composite Action within Reusable Workflow from called workflow repo', #123261 'Using a composite action from a reusable workflow', #25289 'Calling custom composite action from reusable workflow', #11771). The consistent, practitioner-confirmed behavior: `uses: ./.github/actions/foo` is not a git fetch -- it just reads files already on disk in $GITHUB_WORKSPACE. Which repo's files are on disk is determined entirely by whatever `actions/checkout` step ran earlier in THAT job, not by which repo the reusable-workflow YAML file lives in. Per GitHub Docs ('Reusing workflow configurations'), inside a called (workflow_call) workflow the `github` context -- including `github.repository` -- reflects the CALLER, not the reusable workflow's own repo (only `github.workflow_ref`/`github.workflow_sha`/`job_workflow_ref` point at the reusable workflow itself). So a bare `actions/checkout` in the reusable workflow checks out the caller's repo@ref. When caller-repo == workflow-owning-repo (the claim's stated scenario: same-repo reusable workflow), this is a distinction without a difference and the local composite action resolves correctly every time -- this is a common, working production pattern (monorepos routinely have workflows/*.yml call local workflows which call local .github/actions/* composite actions). The failure mode practitioners hit again and again, per those threads, is the DIFFERENT scenario: a reusable workflow (with its bundled composite actions) invoked from a DIFFERENT repo -- checkout there pulls the caller's repo, `./.github/actions/foo` doesn't exist there, and CI dies with 'Can't find action.yml... Did you forget to run actions/checkout before running your local action?'. The documented workaround is an explicit second checkout with `repository:`/`ref:`/`path:` pointing at the actions-owning repo.
votes.implication: For the Buildroot kernel-leg decision this is same-repo-only (no cross-org reuse in scope), so the cross-repo failure mode does not apply to either A1 or A2 -- this particular claim is a non-issue either way and should NOT be the deciding factor. What actually differentiates A1 vs A2 in practice: (1) Composite actions are step-bundles only -- action.yml has no `strategy`, `runs-on`, `permissions`, `needs`, `if`, or `timeout-minutes` keys, so a composite action at .github/actions/kernel-leg/ CANNOT itself carry the matrix strategy for the 4h build; every calling workflow would still have to re-declare the matrixed job wrapper (runs-on + strategy.matrix + artifact-upload step invoking the action), meaning A1 only dedups step logic, not the job/matrix definition that is the actual duplication being complained about. A2 (workflow_call) can declare `strategy: matrix:` once inside the reusable workflow plus `outputs:`, giving true job-level dedup -- the stronger fit for 'the shared job is a matrixed 4h build.' (2) Neither `uses: ./.github/actions/kernel-leg` nor `uses: ./.github/workflows/kernel-leg.yml` can carry an `@<sha>` pin -- local path references are implicitly pinned by the caller's own checked-out commit, so the repo's 'pin every action by SHA' policy is naturally satisfied (not violated) by either choice for the local wrapper itself; SHA-pinning still applies normally to any third-party `uses:` steps nested inside whichever wrapper is picked. (3) Branch-protection required check: keep the existing dedicated 'status' aggregation job living IN the top-level calling workflow with `needs: [kernel-leg]` reading `needs.kernel-leg.result`, regardless of whether kernel-leg is A1's job or A2's called-workflow job -- this sidesteps the known GH UI wrinkle where individual matrix-leg/called-workflow check-run names are less stable/discoverable to select directly as a required check than a single job living in the caller. (4) Artifact handoff to the downstream job in the same run is a non-differentiator: upload-artifact/download-artifact are scoped to the whole run, not to job or workflow-file boundaries, so it works identically whether the producing job came from a composite-action-wrapped job (A1) or a workflow_call'd job (A2). Net: given the matrix requirement specifically, A2 (reusable workflow) is the better structural fit; A1 would still leave the matrix strategy duplicated across every caller, only partially solving the stated duplication problem.
votes.caveats: Holds only because caller repo and workflow-owning repo are identical here; if this reusable workflow or its composite actions are ever invoked from a different repo (e.g. a future shared org template), the local `uses: ./...` path breaks silently with a checkout-related error and needs an explicit extra `actions/checkout` with `repository:`/`ref:`/`path:` pointing at the actions-owning repo. Also: every job (including the reusable workflow's own job and the downstream artifact-consuming job) still needs its own `actions/checkout` step -- workspace state is not inherited between jobs regardless of the A1/A2 choice.

claim: Jobs from a called reusable workflow appear as check runs named "<caller-job> / <called-job>", which changes the names branch protection matches against.

votes.lens: Official GitHub Actions documentation and changelogs (strict)
votes.evidence: I fetched and text-searched the official pages most likely to state this: "Reuse workflows" (docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows — all 13 section headings, plus targeted re-fetches of "Calling a reusable workflow" and "Nesting reusable workflows"), "Reusing workflow configurations" (reference page), "Workflow syntax for GitHub Actions" (the jobs.<job_id>.name entry — only states the name is "displayed in the GitHub UI", nothing about reusable-workflow composition), "Troubleshooting required status checks" (explicitly confirmed the string "reusable" does not appear anywhere on that page), and the 2021 GA changelog post for reusable workflows. None of these official pages contain a sentence describing check-run names being composed as "<caller-job> / <called-job>" or any caller/called naming concatenation. A WebSearch call initially produced a confident-sounding paraphrase ("the name format for status checks is <job name> / <reusable job name>"), but a direct fetch-and-quote check of that specific doc page proved the phrase is NOT actually present in the source — it was the search tool's own synthesis from secondary/community sources, not a documented fact. The underlying behavior IS real and widely reported in GitHub's own Community Discussions forum (e.g. discussions #46752, #170628, #31054 — users and GitHub staff replies describing exactly "validate / validate" and nested "CI / CI / Commit Validator" check names, and confirming branch protection must be reconfigured to match), but a community forum is not "official documentation," and I could not locate a docs page or changelog entry that states this naming rule explicitly.
votes.implication: Per the strict lens, do not treat this as a documented GitHub guarantee — but do not discount it either, since it is consistently and repeatedly confirmed in GitHub's own Community forum with no contradicting reports, so it should be assumed true in practice even though it isn't in the formal docs. Practical effect on your decision: A2 (workflow_call) makes the kernel-leg job's reported check name a composite of the caller job name and the called workflow's job name, so extracting it into a reusable workflow would very likely rename the check(s) GitHub reports, which is exactly the kind of drift you'd want your SHA-pinning/required-check discipline to avoid — it would require you to re-point the branch-protection required-check config to the new composite name(s) at refactor time (and again if you ever rename either job). A1 (composite action at .github/actions/kernel-leg/) has no such risk under any interpretation: a composite action's steps execute inside the existing job, so the job/check name the caller workflow already reports is untouched — your designated aggregation job stays the required check with no renaming, regardless of whether the "<caller>/<called>" pattern is real. Since your matrixed build's only cross-boundary need is "run steps, produce artifacts consumed later in the same run" (not independent triggering/multiple callers/separate versioning), the composite-action route sidesteps this open question entirely and is the lower-risk choice for preserving your required-check name; only prefer A2 if you specifically want the kernel-leg to be independently invocable/reusable across multiple caller workflows, in which case budget an explicit branch-protection required-check update (verified by triggering one real run and reading the actual reported check name post-refactor, since docs won't tell you) as part of that migration.

votes.evidence:
The core mechanism is real and confirmed directly by GitHub Support (quoted in a community thread): "The name of the reported check will capture the names of the jobs in both the caller and called workflow" — e.g. a caller job `validate` calling a reusable workflow job `validate` reports as check `validate / validate`, breaking a branch-protection rule that required `validate` (github.com/orgs/community/discussions/8512). This is confirmed as by-design, persistent behavior, not a bug awaiting a version fix — no changelog entry marks it as changed/fixed.

However, the claim's literal format "<caller-job> / <called-job>" is an oversimplification that fails in exactly the conditions relevant here:
1. Matrix suffixes: when either the caller job or the called job in the reusable workflow uses a `strategy.matrix`, GitHub appends the matrix values in parentheses to the check name (e.g. `build (arch=arm) / kernel` or `build / kernel (arch=arm)`), so the reported name is not the clean two-segment string the claim states. This directly matters here since the job being extracted is matrixed.
2. Nested/chained reusable workflows produce longer, non-two-part chains — one unresolved community report shows a job name rendering as "CI / CI / Commit Validator" instead of the expected "CI / Commit Validator" (github.com/orgs/community/discussions/46752), i.e. the naming isn't reliably just "<caller-job> / <called-job>" once more than one level of workflow_call is involved.
3. It is well documented (and repeatedly reported as a limitation, e.g. discussions #4324 and #26822) that GitHub Actions cannot use an entire matrix or an entire called workflow as a single required check — teams must add a downstream aggregation job and require only that job's check. This is precisely the pattern the repo already uses, which limits (but does not eliminate) the blast radius of the renaming: only the one designated aggregation-job check name needs to be updated in branch protection, not every matrix leg.
4. Composite actions (the A1 alternative) are NOT subject to any of this: a composite action's steps execute inside the existing caller job, so the job/check name is exactly whatever the caller workflow already names it — no rename, no required-check churn, ever.
votes.implication: The claim's directional point is correct enough to act on: adopting a reusable workflow (A2) for the kernel-leg job WILL rename the check that GitHub reports for the aggregation job from "<aggregation-job>" to "<caller-job> / <aggregation-job>", requiring one deliberate, one-time update to the branch-protection required-check list at cutover — not an ongoing burden, since the repo already funnels the matrix into a single required aggregation job rather than requiring individual matrix legs. But do not take the claim's exact string format at face value: if the matrixed build job itself (not just the aggregation job) is ever inspected in required-checks UI, its per-leg names will carry matrix-value suffixes and could grow into multi-segment chains if the reusable workflow is nested further (e.g. called by another reusable workflow), which is an open, unresolved GitHub limitation with no clean workaround beyond "match whatever string the UI actually shows." Net effect: A2 costs exactly one branch-protection required-check rename (low, one-time, verifiable in a dry-run PR before merging) and is not the parade-of-breakage the raw claim implies; A1 (composite action) avoids this class of risk entirely since composite-action steps never spawn a separately named check. This is a real but bounded and one-time cost for A2, not a blocker — weigh it against A2's other advantages (native reusable-workflow features like `secrets: inherit`, workflow-level `permissions`, and cleaner separation of the ~4h job's own trigger/timeout/concurrency config) versus A1's simplicity (no separate check-name migration, but composite actions cannot define their own job-level `timeout-minutes`, `runs-on`, or `permissions` — those stay dictated by the calling job, which matters for a 4h matrixed build).

votes.evidence: The naming mechanic itself is confirmed by GitHub's own community support channel and independently corroborated across multiple threads: a job that calls a reusable workflow gets its check runs reported as "<caller-job-name> / <called-job-name>", and branch protection has to be pointed at that exact concatenated string, not the individual job name (GitHub Community Discussion #46752, #8512, #12395, #29825, #56317, #170628; devactivity.com writeup). Real-world failure modes confirmed in practice, not just theory: (1) nesting — calling a reusable workflow that itself calls another reusable workflow compounds the prefix (observed case: "CI / CI / Commit Validator" instead of the expected "CI / Commit Validator"), silently breaking a branch-protection rule that was configured for the shallower name (#46752); GitHub's docs cap nesting at 10 levels total, but say nothing about taming the resulting name depth. (2) matrix strategies append the matrix key to the check name too (e.g. "job / called-job (leg-name)"), and GitHub Community Discussion #26822/#60792 confirm you cannot cleanly require individual matrix-leg checks — teams' standard workaround is exactly a downstream aggregator job with `needs: [...]` and `if: always()` that gates on its own single name, because `needs:` resolves against workflow-graph job IDs, not check-run display strings, so the aggregator's own required-check name is unaffected by whatever compounding happens inside the jobs it depends on. (3) People do get bitten in production by silent required-check mismatches after an internal job/workflow rename, because GitHub requires an exact string match with no aliasing.
votes.implication: For this repo specifically, the claim is real but largely defanged by the design already in place: since `status` is a plain job that lives in the top-level caller workflow and aggregates the matrixed kernel-build job via `needs:`, its own required-check name never changes regardless of whether the kernel-build leg is a composite action (A1) or a called reusable workflow (A2) — `needs:` tracks job IDs, not the "<caller>/<called>" check-run string, so branch protection on `status` keeps working either way. That means checkrun-naming is not, by itself, a hard blocker for A2. It is still a real argument in favor of A1: a composite action runs inline inside the existing job with zero naming indirection, no nesting-depth bookkeeping, and no extra compound-named entries cluttering the PR Checks tab for a 4-hour matrixed build; A2 would add one "kernel-leg-caller / build (matrix-value)" entry per leg, which is cosmetic today but becomes a live footgun the moment anyone later tries to require an individual leg (e.g. "block merge if the RT leg fails") — at that point they hit the documented exact-string-match fragility and nesting-depth compounding, and a future rename of the called job/workflow will silently desync branch protection with no warning. Net: pick A2 only if the team commits to never requiring anything below `status`, and keeps `status` itself out of any called workflow; if there's real chance of wanting per-leg required checks later, or of nesting this workflow inside another reusable workflow down the line, A1's flat, rename-safe naming is the lower-surprise choice given this repo already leans toward explicit, stable pinning (SHA-pinned actions) as a design value.
votes.caveats: The mismatch risk is conditional: it only bites required checks that target job names living inside a called reusable workflow (individual matrix legs) or that sit two-plus levels of workflow nesting deep. A top-level aggregator job (this repo's `status`) that depends on the called job via `needs:` and is itself required is immune to the naming string, because branch protection is matching on `status`'s own unchanged check-run name, not on anything produced inside the workflow it depends on. Also worth flagging separately: GitHub's newer repository Rulesets can match required checks by workflow/integration source rather than exact job-name string, which would sidestep this whole class of problem if the repo migrates off classic branch-protection required-status-checks — not confirmed whether this repo uses classic branch protection vs. rulesets, so this alternate mitigation is unverified for their setup.

claim: A job that uses `uses: ./.github/workflows/x.yml` can also carry `strategy: matrix`, and each matrix leg calls the workflow separately.

votes.evidence:
GitHub's "Reuse workflows" documentation (docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows, mirrored at .../using-workflows/reusing-workflows) explicitly documents this pattern under a matrix-strategy section. It states: "A matrix strategy lets you use variables in a single job definition to automatically create multiple job runs that are based on the combinations of the variables," and gives this exact example:

  jobs:
    ReusableMatrixJobForDeployment:
      strategy:
        matrix:
          target: [dev, stage, prod]
      uses: octocat/octo-repo/.github/workflows/deployment.yml@main
      with:
        target: ${{ matrix.target }}

The docs then say this "will run three jobs, one for each value in the variable" — i.e., each matrix leg is a separate call of the reusable workflow (each becomes its own workflow run, invoked with matrix.target substituted into `with:`). This applies identically to local-path callees (`./.github/workflows/x.yml`), since local and remote/pinned reusable-workflow references use the same `uses:` job-level syntax. No limitation on combining matrix with reusable-workflow calls is documented (fail-fast/max-parallel behave as with any matrix job).
votes.implication:
This directly enables A2 (reusable workflow with workflow_call) as a clean way to matrix the ~4h kernel-build leg: the caller job carries `strategy: matrix` and `uses: ./.github/workflows/kernel-leg.yml@<sha-or-ref>`, producing one separate run per matrix leg, each with its own job-level status check name (e.g. "kernel-leg / build (arm)") — which matters for branch-protection required-check design, since each matrix leg surfaces as a distinct check rather than being collapsed into one. By contrast, A1 (composite action) has no independent job-level matrix semantics of its own — matrixing a composite action requires wrapping it in an ordinary job's `strategy: matrix`, which is also supported and simpler, but keeps all legs as steps within one job (one status check, not several), and composite actions cannot themselves consume `secrets:`/have their own `permissions:`/run their own `runs-on:` per leg the way a called reusable workflow's job can. If the design wants the aggregation job to depend on N distinct required per-arch checks, A2 (matrix + workflow_call) is the pattern GitHub's docs bless directly; if a single consolidated check covering all legs is preferred, a matrixed job wrapping a composite action (A1) is simpler and avoids the cross-workflow indirection (separate artifact-upload/download semantics between calling and called workflows).</implication>
</invoke>

votes.evidence: Current GitHub docs (docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows, section 'Using a matrix strategy with a reusable workflow') confirm the core mechanic: 'Jobs using the matrix strategy can call a reusable workflow' — a job with strategy.matrix + uses: ./.github/workflows/x.yml runs one separate call/job per matrix combination (docs' own example: 3 matrix values -> 3 job runs). This was NOT always true: matrix support for jobs that call reusable workflows was added as part of the 'reusable workflows GA' rollout (~Nov 2022); older GHES doc snapshots (e.g. GHES 3.4, 2022) still state 'The strategy property is not supported in any job that calls a reusable workflow' — a real historical version gate, moot only because it's github.com in 2026. More importantly, the SAME current docs page states a load-bearing exception directly relevant to this repo's 'later job in the same run consumes it' pattern: 'If a reusable workflow that sets an output is executed with a matrix strategy, the output will be the output set by the last successful completing reusable workflow of the matrix which actually sets a value' — i.e. per-leg outputs are NOT aggregated across matrix legs; only one (nondeterministic-order) leg's output survives, silently discarding the rest. I also confirmed via the workflow-syntax reference (jobs.<job_id>.uses) that local reusable-workflow calls ('./.github/workflows/{filename}') explicitly forbid an @{ref}/SHA suffix and forbid contexts/expressions in that keyword — a local `uses:` is always implicitly pinned to the caller's own commit, it cannot itself carry a SHA pin the way the repo pins other actions.
votes.implication: The mechanical premise checks out well enough to unblock A2 (reusable workflow with workflow_call): a matrixed kernel-leg job CAN call ./.github/workflows/kernel-leg.yml and will fan out into separate per-leg job runs today, on github.com, no version gate applies. But two real exceptions must shape the design, not be assumed away: (1) Never route per-leg data (artifact name, cache key, arch tag) through workflow_call `outputs` when the calling job is matrixed — GitHub only keeps one leg's output, so the downstream artifact-consuming job would nondeterministically see just one leg's value. Since the described design already uses artifact upload/download (not outputs) to hand data to the later job, this is survivable, but only if artifact names are deterministically derived from the matrix key (e.g. `kernel-${{ matrix.arch }}`) rather than passed back as a job output — call this out explicitly in the implementation so nobody 'simplifies' it into an output later. (2) Matrixed calls to a reusable workflow produce per-leg check names that embed the matrix value, so branch protection still cannot point at an individual leg — the existing plan to gate on a dedicated aggregation job (as already noted in the RT-kernel memory: 'serial gate→build-kernel[matrix]→build') remains the right shape for either A1 or A2; this claim doesn't change that decision. (3) The SHA-pinning policy ('pins every action by SHA') is a non-differentiator between A1 and A2 for this specific extraction: a local composite action (`uses: ./.github/actions/kernel-leg`) and a local reusable workflow (`uses: ./.github/workflows/kernel-leg.yml`) are BOTH inherently same-commit and neither can carry an @sha — that policy only bites for calls into other repos, so it shouldn't be weighed as evidence for either option.
votes.caveats: Verdict is PARTIAL rather than REFUTED because the core structural claim (matrix + local workflow_call = N separate calls) is true today and would need github.com's actual GA rollout timeline (~Nov 2022) to fail, which is not this repo's situation. It's PARTIAL rather than CONFIRMED because (a) the claim is stated as an unconditional fact when it was, in living memory, a documented unsupported combination pre-GA, and (b) the docs themselves flag a genuine, easy-to-hit correctness trap (matrix output collapse to 'last leg wins') that directly threatens the exact 'later job in the same run consumes it' pattern this decision is about, if implemented carelessly via outputs instead of named artifacts. Also unverified in this pass: whether GHES (self-hosted Enterprise Server) is in play for this repo — if it is, that specific GHES version's docs snapshot should be checked, since older GHES releases can still ship the pre-GA 'strategy not supported' restriction baked into that version's docs even though github.com has moved on.

votes.evidence: GitHub's docs (docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows) state plainly: "Jobs using the matrix strategy can call a reusable workflow... you can use a matrix strategy to pass different inputs to a reusable workflow." Each matrix leg does spawn a separate invocation of the called workflow (shown as a nested job group in the UI), and this is a widely-used, documented pattern — not a hack. That said, real-world use surfaces friction the docs gloss over: (1) matrix context is NOT automatically visible inside the callee — each leg's matrix value must be threaded explicitly through `with:` inputs on the caller job; (2) job outputs from a matrix-strategy reusable-workflow call follow the same GitHub-wide matrix-output rule as any matrix job — only the last-completing leg's output value survives (community discussions confirm this bites people specifically in the reusable-workflow case, forcing artifact-based workarounds via e.g. cloudposse/github-action-matrix-outputs-{write,read}); (3) actions/runner#2475 ("Job Interference using Reusable Workflows with Matrix Strategy") reported jobs from one matrix leg's reusable-workflow invocation leaking/appearing under another leg after UI re-runs of failed jobs — an isolation bug specific to the matrix+reusable-workflow combination that doesn't exist for composite actions, since composite actions never create a separate workflow invocation to isolate in the first place. Composite actions, by contrast, only ever execute as inline steps inside whatever job (matrixed or not) references them — a much older, more mechanically simple, and more heavily-trodden pattern with no analogous isolation-bug class.
votes.implication: Go with A1 (composite action at .github/actions/kernel-leg/), not A2. The claim being true doesn't make A2 the better fit here: with a composite action, matrixing the existing kernel-build job is unchanged from today's topology — one job, N legs, each leg's steps run inline on its own runner, each leg uploads its own artifact, the downstream job and the required-check aggregation `status` job depend on the matrixed job exactly as they presumably do now via `needs:` and artifact download — zero new failure surface. A2 would restructure that single job into a caller-job-plus-nested-reusable-workflow-invocation per leg, which buys nothing here (the shared unit is one job, not a multi-job DAG, and nothing outside this repo needs to call it), while importing two real risks that matter for a 4h build: the last-writer-wins matrix-output gotcha (irrelevant if you're only passing artifacts, but a trap if you ever want per-leg metadata like kernel version/hash via `needs.*.outputs`) and the actions/runner#2475-style isolation bug that specifically appears on re-runs of failed matrix legs — exactly the scenario a flaky 4-hour kernel build will produce often. SHA-pinning policy is a non-issue either way since both `uses: ./.github/actions/kernel-leg` and `uses: ./.github/workflows/kernel-leg.yml` are same-repo relative-path references, not third-party actions requiring SHA pins.
votes.caveats: The isolation bug (actions/runner#2475) was reported March 2023 and its current reproducibility in 2026 is unverified — treat it as evidence of extra edge-case surface in the matrix+reusable-workflow combination rather than a guaranteed current blocker. The output-aggregation limitation applies to GitHub Actions matrix jobs generally (not unique to reusable workflows) but is more likely to be hit when the callee is a whole reusable workflow with its own declared `outputs:` block, since that's the mechanism reusable workflows use to surface results to the caller. None of this affects artifact upload/download across job boundaries, which works identically for both A1 and A2 since artifacts are run-scoped, not job- or invocation-scoped.

claim: Workflow-level and job-level `env:` do NOT propagate into a called reusable workflow; values must be passed as explicit `with:` inputs.

votes.evidence: GitHub's official docs page "Reusing workflow configurations" (docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/reusing-workflow-configurations) states verbatim: "Any environment variables set in an env context defined at the workflow level in the caller workflow are not propagated to the called workflow." It further states the reverse is also blocked: "Similarly, environment variables set in the env context, defined in the called workflow, are not accessible in the env context of the caller workflow." The docs' prescribed alternative is explicit: pass values via `with:` inputs into the reusable workflow's declared `on.workflow_call.inputs`, and get values back out via the reusable workflow's declared `outputs:` (not via GITHUB_ENV) — "Reusable workflows are called directly within a job, and not from within a job step. You cannot, therefore, use GITHUB_ENV to pass values to job steps in the caller workflow." The separate "Reuse workflows" how-to page corroborates the general pattern for inputs/secrets: "Use the with keyword in a job to pass named inputs... Use the secrets keyword to pass named secrets," with `secrets: inherit` as the only implicit-passing exception, and that exception applies only to secrets, not env/vars.
votes.implication: This is a real, docs-confirmed constraint that cuts specifically against A2 (workflow_call) for this job, not against A1. If any of the matrixed kernel-build job's current behavior depends on workflow-level or job-level env: vars set in the calling workflow (e.g. cache keys, toolchain versions, artifact names, BR2_* overrides) leaking in implicitly, converting to a reusable workflow will silently break that on refactor — every one of those has to be re-declared as a formal `inputs:` (and any needed values coming back out re-declared as `outputs:`), which is a real migration cost and a source of subtle bugs if missed. A composite action (A1) does not have this restriction in the same way: composite actions execute as steps inside the caller's own job, so they DO see the job's env context directly (the caller's env is simply the environment the composite's steps run in) — no explicit re-plumbing of env vars is needed, only true action inputs need `with:`. Given the repo pins every action by SHA and has a specific aggregation job as the required status check, and the shared piece is one job's steps (build + artifact upload) rather than multiple jobs with their own runners/matrix-fanout-plus-downstream-job graph, the env-propagation gap is a concrete point in favor of A1 (composite action) unless the team is prepared to audit and explicitly re-pass every env var the kernel-build job currently relies on. If workflow_call is still preferred (e.g., for its native matrix: support at the job level, or because a fully separate job in the check graph is desired), plan the input/output interface explicitly and do not assume any ambient env: values will cross the boundary.

votes.evidence: GitHub's own docs (Reusing workflow configurations / Access rights of reusable workflows) state verbatim: "Any environment variables set in an env context defined at the workflow level in the caller workflow are not propagated to the called workflow" and "Environment variables set in the env context, defined in the called workflow, are not accessible in the env context of the caller workflow." No version gate, changelog, or runner update reversing this was found — it's an open community complaint since 2022+ (discussions #26671, #77259, #25238, github/docs issue #25620) with no GitHub-staff fix confirmed (one unverified April-2025 comment claims it "may have been resolved" but is uncorroborated and contradicted by current docs). Further, `env:` is not even a legal key on a job whose only content is `uses:` (calling a reusable workflow) — that job schema accepts only a narrow keyword set (needs/if/permissions/name/uses/with/secrets/strategy); `continue-on-error` is rejected the same way per community discussion #77915 — so the restriction is schema-enforced, not a silent no-op. HOWEVER the claim's prescriptive half ("must be passed as explicit with: inputs") overstates the only remedy: (a) repository/organization/environment-scoped Configuration Variables (the `vars` context, Settings > Variables) ARE automatically visible inside a called reusable workflow with zero `with:` plumbing, since they belong to the repo/org scope rather than the caller workflow's `env:` block; (b) GitHub Environment-scoped secrets/vars are similarly auto-available if the reusable workflow's job declares a matching `environment:`, no `with:` needed; (c) secrets specifically have `secrets: inherit`, a non-`with:` propagation path. So the core technical fact (workflow/job env: does not flow into workflow_call) is solid and exception-free; the "therefore everything must go through with: inputs" framing is a simplification that ignores the vars/environment-scope escape hatches.
votes.implication: For the ~4h matrixed kernel-build job, this cuts toward A1 (composite action) if the job leans on ambient workflow/job-level env (toolchain versions, cache keys, kernel version, ccache paths, etc.): a composite action executes inline in the caller's job/runner process and inherits that env automatically — no refactor needed. Choosing A2 (reusable workflow) means every such env value must be enumerated as an explicit `with:` input, or hoisted to repo/org Configuration Variables (`vars` context) which the called workflow can read natively without any `with:` plumbing — a real but bounded one-time migration, and it buys an explicit, self-documenting input contract plus first-class job identity/status for the required-check aggregation job, which a composite action (no independent job) can't give branch protection to gate on directly. Given the repo pins everything by SHA and clearly wants an explicit required-check boundary, the `with:`-input discipline A2 forces is arguably a feature — but before committing, audit the current duplicated job's env surface and see how much of it can move to repo-level `vars` instead of per-call `with:`, since that sidesteps most of the migration churn implied by the claim.

votes.evidence: GitHub's own docs and practitioner reports agree: `env:` set at workflow or job level in a caller does NOT flow into a `workflow_call`-invoked reusable workflow. The called workflow only ever sees the `github`/`needs` contexts plus whatever is explicitly declared under `workflow_call.inputs` and passed via `with:` (and secrets via explicit `secrets:` entries or `secrets: inherit`, which itself is hop-by-hop, not transitive through a 3-workflow chain unless each link re-declares it). Community discussion #26671 (github.com/orgs/community/discussions/26671) shows this bites real teams constantly — "the environment from main workflow does not extend to the reusable workflow it calls" — with the standard workaround being an extra setup job that turns env values into `outputs` and feeds them through `with:`. One frequently-confused nuance: repo/org/environment *configuration variables* (`vars.*`) ARE automatically visible inside a called reusable workflow (they're fetched from repo settings, not injected via the `env:` YAML block), so teams sometimes wrongly conclude "variables work fine" from testing `vars.FOO` and then get burned when `env.FOO` (workflow-level) silently comes back empty in the called workflow. Separately, I verified via GitHub's reuse-workflows docs that a job calling a reusable workflow with `uses:` CAN carry a `strategy.matrix` (this has been supported since 2022) — so matrix is not a discriminator between the two designs, despite some outdated blog posts claiming otherwise.
votes.implication:
Confirmed env non-propagation is a real migration cost, and it cuts toward A1 (composite action) for this specific job. Buildroot kernel-build jobs are almost always parameter-heavy (KERNEL_VERSION, BR2_DEFCONFIG, ccache/output paths, toolchain selection, matrix arch/variant, etc.), and if today's duplicated job leans on workflow- or job-level `env:` for any of that, A2 (reusable workflow) forces you to enumerate every one of those as an explicit `workflow_call.inputs` entry and rewrite every call site's `with:` block — real, error-prone surface area for a 4h build where a silently-empty input means you discover the break only after burning 4 hours of matrixed CI. A1 sidesteps this entirely: a composite action's steps execute inside the calling job, so it inherits that job's (and the workflow's) `env:` context for free — zero plumbing, zero risk of a quietly-empty variable.

The other two factors you flagged turn out to be non-issues either way: (1) matrix — both designs support `strategy: matrix` at the job that invokes the shared logic, so the 4h matrixed fan-out works identically under A1 or A2; (2) artifact handoff to the downstream job — artifact storage is scoped to the whole workflow *run*, not to which job/composite-action/reusable-workflow uploaded it, so `actions/download-artifact` in the downstream job works the same regardless of which path you choose; (3) SHA-pinning — since this is an intra-repo shared job (`./.github/actions/...` or `./.github/workflows/...`), both are implicitly pinned to the same ref/SHA as the caller, so your "pin everything by SHA" policy isn't a differentiator here (it would only bite if you later externalized the shared job to a separate repo, in which case A2's `uses: org/repo/.github/workflows/x.yml@<sha>` is the cleaner, more idiomatic pinning target than a cross-repo composite action).

The one place A2 could still bite is your branch-protection required check: GitHub reports reusable-workflow call results as a concatenated "caller-job / called-job" check name, which has tripped people up when renaming jobs. Since you're routing branch protection through a dedicated `status` aggregation job (not requiring the matrix/kernel-leg job directly), this risk is largely absorbed — just make sure `status` depends on the *outer* job id via `needs:`, not on a check name string, and you're insulated from that naming quirk under either design.

Net: given the described job is already-duplicated, env-var-heavy, single-repo, and its required-check path already goes through an aggregator, A1 (composite action) is the lower-friction, lower-risk refactor — it needs no env→inputs migration. Choose A2 only if you also want the reusable workflow's other real advantages (a self-documenting, explicitly-declared `secrets:` contract instead of stuffing credentials into string `with:` inputs on a composite action; and cleaner separate job/log entries per matrix leg in the Actions UI) — but budget explicit time to inventory and re-plumb every env-derived value as a declared input, and test that the branch-protection check still resolves correctly after the switch.

claim: From the caller, `needs.<reusable-job>.result` behaves the same as for a normal job (success/failure/skipped/cancelled), so a status-aggregation job keeps working unchanged.

votes.lens: Official GitHub Actions documentation and changelogs (strict)
votes.evidence:
GitHub's Contexts reference defines the property generically, with no carve-out for jobs that call a reusable workflow: "needs.<job_id>.result | string | The result of a job that the current job depends on. Possible values are success, failure, cancelled, or skipped." (https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/contexts, table under the `needs` context section). Separately, the Workflow syntax reference documents `jobs.<job_id>.uses` — "The location and version of a reusable workflow file to run as a job" — as just one more key on the same `jobs.<job_id>` schema that also carries `needs`, `if`, `runs-on`, etc. (https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax). Nothing on either page states or implies that a job's `result` semantics change when the job body is `uses: <reusable-workflow>` instead of `runs-on/steps`; the reusable-workflow-calling job is structurally an ordinary node in the `needs` graph. I could not find any GitHub changelog entry stating a change to this behavior.

Two caveats surfaced during verification that are NOT resolved by official docs (flagging per the strict-verification mandate, not folding them into the docs' silence):
1. actions/runner issue #2495 ("Reusable workflow doesn't output the individual job `result`", open since 2023) — this is a DIFFERENT mechanism (`jobs.<job_id>.result` used *inside* the reusable workflow's `workflow_call.outputs` block to re-expose a nested job's result) and does not describe the caller-side `needs.<job>.result` the claim is about; I flag it only so it isn't confused with the claim.
2. GitHub community discussion #123803 (open, zero replies, not acknowledged by GitHub staff) reports an edge case where `needs.<job_calling_reusable_workflow>.result` reads "success" when the call actually failed due to a `fromJson()` input-parsing error in the reusable workflow's inputs. This is an unverified, unofficial bug report — not documented in GitHub's docs or changelog — so it does not overturn the documented general behavior, but it is a known reliability gap for one specific failure mode (bad `fromJson()` input) that a status-aggregation job cannot fully rule out via docs alone.
3. Community discussion #8512 (GitHub Support reply) confirms that when a job's *body* is `uses: <reusable-workflow>`, the required-status-check name GitHub reports for THAT job becomes "callerJob / calledJob" (concatenated), not the plain job name — relevant only if that job itself, rather than a separate aggregator, were made the required check.
votes.implication: This resolves the main risk premise for choosing A2 (reusable workflow via workflow_call): converting the duplicated kernel-build job into a `uses: .github/workflows/kernel-leg.yml` call does not require rewriting the aggregation job's logic. `needs.<kernel-leg-job>.result` will still take the ordinary success/failure/cancelled/skipped values per the documented, job-type-agnostic definition, so any `if: contains(needs.*.result, 'failure')`-style gate in the required-check aggregation job keeps working unchanged — this is not a blocker for A2. Caveat #3 (check-name concatenation) is irrelevant to your current design since the required check is the separate aggregation job, not the kernel-leg-calling job itself — but avoid ever repointing branch protection directly at a job whose body is `uses:` a reusable workflow, since its reported check name changes shape. Caveat #2 (unresolved, unofficial `fromJson()` edge-case bug) argues for keeping (or adding) an explicit `if: always()` + `contains(needs.*.result, 'failure')`/`cancelled()` check in the aggregator rather than trusting a bare `needs.kernel-leg.result == 'success'`/implicit-success gate, regardless of whether you pick A1 or A2 — cheap insurance either way. For A1 (composite action), none of this applies or matters: a composite action has no jobs of its own and runs as steps inside the caller's single job, so there is no `needs` context or job-level `result` semantics to worry about — the whole question is moot under A1, and status aggregation stays exactly as it is today.

votes.evidence: The baseline claim holds for a *single, non-matrixed* job that does `uses: ./.github/workflows/x.yml` — `needs.<job>.result` in a downstream job does resolve to success/failure/skipped/cancelled as documented. But there is a well-documented, reproducible exception that lands squarely on this repo's exact scenario (a *matrixed* job wrapped in a reusable workflow feeding a downstream aggregation job): GitHub Community Discussion #63860, "Workflow run status is `canceled` when fail-fasting a matrix of reusable workflows" (https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/63860). When a matrix strategy calls a reusable workflow per-leg with the default `fail-fast: true`, a failing leg causes sibling legs to be reported as `canceled` rather than the `failed` you get with an equivalent inline (non-reusable) matrix job — the runner logs "FailFast: cancelling since parallel instance has failed" but surfaces it differently for workflow_call legs. Per the discussion, this breaks downstream detection: "neither `failure()` nor `cancelled()` conditions activate properly, preventing downstream aggregation jobs from detecting the failure state." A second, compounding bug (actions/runner#2495, https://github.com/actions/runner/issues/2495, and #3087) shows `jobs.<job_id>.result` referenced from inside a reusable workflow's own `on.workflow_call.outputs` resolves to an empty string unless wrapped in `toJSON()`/`fromJSON()` — relevant if the aggregation logic leans on the called workflow re-exporting its own job status. Community discussion #47243 ("Pass job status down to reusable workflow") independently confirms a contributor's warning that wrapping a caller as a reusable workflow surfaces "strange bugs" around result/status propagation, with no official GitHub staff rebuttal found in any of these threads. None of the three threads show a GitHub-confirmed fix or version gate — they read as an open edge case as of this search (July 2026), not something resolved by a runner/workflow-syntax version bump.
votes.implication: Because the real job is matrixed, promoting it to a reusable workflow (A2) called via a matrix strategy imports a real, documented failure mode: a failing leg can surface to `needs.<kernel-leg>.result` as `cancelled` instead of `failure` (default fail-fast), which the existing status-aggregation job — the one branch protection requires — would need to special-case (check both `failure` and `cancelled`, or force `fail-fast: false` and accept messier per-leg reasoning) to avoid silently going green or silently blocking merges for the wrong reason. That's exactly the kind of subtle regression you don't want landing in a required check. A composite action (A1) sidesteps the whole class of bug: it runs as steps inside the *existing* matrixed job rather than as a separate workflow_call-bounded job, so there is no independent reusable-workflow job result to reconcile — the current matrix + aggregation-job `needs.*.result` semantics keep working completely unchanged, with zero new edge cases to test. If A2 is still preferred (e.g., for artifact-upload/download boundary clarity across the ~4h build), the team should explicitly set `strategy.fail-fast: false` on the calling matrix and treat `cancelled` as a possible false-negative-for-failure state in the aggregation job's condition, and should test-cancel one leg deliberately before trusting it as the required check.

votes.evidence:
The base claim holds for the common path: `needs.<job>.result` on a job that calls a reusable workflow does report success/failure/skipped/cancelled, and "matrix leg → wrapper/status job reading needs.*.result → single required check" is exactly the pattern GitHub's own community repeatedly recommends as the workaround for the fact that per-matrix and per-reusable-workflow checks otherwise get ugly composite names in branch protection (github.com/orgs/community/discussions/26822, /4324, /46752, /12395). Artifact upload/download across the workflow_call boundary also just works — artifacts are scoped to the run, not the job graph shape, so a downstream job can pull what the matrixed kernel-build job(s) produced regardless of A1 vs A2.

But "behaves the same as a normal job" is not fully true, and the gaps are documented in production bug reports, not theoretical:
1. A job with `uses:` (calling a reusable workflow) cannot use `continue-on-error` at the job level — GitHub rejects it with a validation error ("Property continue-on-error is not allowed"), forcing error-masking workarounds inside the called workflow instead (github.com/orgs/community/discussions/77915). A normal job or a composite-action step has no such restriction.
2. There is an open, unacknowledged bug (filed May 2024, still unanswered) where `needs.<job_calling_reusable_workflow>.result` reports "success" even though the call effectively failed, when the `with:`/`fromJson()` input expression to the reusable workflow is malformed (e.g., references a wrong/missing key) — the parse failure doesn't propagate as `failure` the way a normal job's step failure would (github.com/orgs/community/discussions/123803).
3. Getting a called-workflow's own job-level `.result` back out through `on.workflow_call.outputs` (e.g., `jobs.<job_id>.result`) is broken/empty (actions/runner#2495, actions/runner#3087) — so any design that tries to surface finer-grained per-leg pass/fail as an output (beyond the built-in aggregate `needs.<job>.result`) doesn't work like it would with normal jobs or a composite action's step outputs.
4. Cancellation semantics around reusable workflows + concurrency/matrix have multiple open community reports of surprising behavior (`cancelled()` returning false after a cancel in matrix-of-reusable-workflow-calls; concurrency-group interactions cancelling more/less than expected) — github.com/orgs/community/discussions/30708, actions/runner#3041. For a 4-hour matrixed build, mid-run cancellations (superseded pushes via `concurrency:`) are routine, not edge cases, so this is a real practical risk to the status job's reliability.
votes.implication:
For THIS repo's shape — SHA-pinned actions, a single `status` job as the branch-protection required check, a ~4h matrixed kernel build whose artifacts feed a same-run downstream job — lean toward the composite action (A1) unless you specifically need workflow_call's cross-workflow-file/cross-repo callability or its secrets/permissions-scoping boundary. A1 sidesteps every gap above by construction: a composite action is just steps glued into the existing job, so there is no separate job node, `needs.<job>.result` is the literal normal-job result (no `continue-on-error` restriction, no `.result`-via-outputs indirection, no reusable-workflow-specific cancellation semantics to worry about), and the matrix stays a plain `strategy.matrix` on one job — the best-understood, most battle-tested GitHub Actions status/branch-protection pattern that all those community threads converge on as "just require the wrapper job."

If A2 is chosen anyway (e.g., because the kernel-leg build needs to be called from more than one workflow file, or needs its own `secrets:`/`permissions:` scoping independent of the caller), the `needs.kernel-leg.result` → `status` job aggregation will work for the mainline success/failure/skipped path, but: (a) do not rely on `continue-on-error` on the job that has `uses:` — it's rejected outright; (b) do not try to thread a finer-grained per-matrix-leg result back through `workflow_call` outputs — test it first, it has reported-broken cases; (c) explicitly test what happens when a superseded run is cancelled mid-4-hour-build under your `concurrency:` group before trusting the `status` job to reflect `cancelled` correctly for branch protection; (d) keep validating `with:` inputs strictly, since malformed-input failures have a reported path to silently reporting `success` in `needs.<job>.result` rather than `failure`.
votes.caveats: Bug #123803 (malformed-input → false success) is a single unverified/unanswered community report, not a GitHub-confirmed defect — treat it as "plausible sharp edge to test for," not settled fact. The `continue-on-error` restriction (#77915) and the workflow_call-outputs `.result` limitation (runner#2495/#3087) are more solid since they're either directly reproducible validation errors or filed against the actions/runner repo itself. All of this is about the CALLING job's status semantics in the SAME run; it is orthogonal to (and does not fix or worsen) the separate, well-known branch-protection check-naming friction for reusable workflows' inner jobs — which doesn't bite here anyway because the required check is the top-level `status` job, not an inner job of the reusable workflow.

claim: A composite action can itself contain `uses:` steps calling actions/upload-artifact, actions/download-artifact and actions/cache.

votes.lens: Official GitHub Actions documentation (docs.github.com) + GitHub changelog
votes.evidence: GitHub's official metadata-syntax reference for composite actions (docs.github.com/en/actions/sharing-automations/creating-actions/metadata-syntax-for-github-actions, section `runs.steps[*].uses`) defines: "**Optional** Selects an action to run as part of a step in your job. An action is a reusable unit of code." and shows this directly under `runs: using: "composite" / steps:` with worked examples such as `- uses: actions/checkout@8f4b7f8...` and a named step `- name: My first step / uses: actions/hello_world@main / with: ...`. This is the generic mechanism — the schema does not enumerate or restrict which published actions may be called this way, so actions/upload-artifact, actions/download-artifact and actions/cache (ordinary marketplace actions referenced the same way, e.g. `owner/repo@sha`) are callable exactly like actions/checkout is in the doc's own example. This capability's introduction is corroborated by the GitHub changelog "Reduce duplication with action composition" (2021-08-25): "Previously, actions written in YAML could only use scripts. Now, they can also reference other actions." — i.e. composite (formerly "composite run steps") actions gained the ability to have `uses:` steps calling other actions, GA'd in that release. No page found states a carve-out for upload-artifact/download-artifact/cache specifically, and none of the docs' "limitations" notes for composite actions (e.g. around outputs, shell requirement on run steps) mention any restriction on which called actions are permitted.
votes.caveats: Docs confirm the general mechanism and give actions/checkout as the worked example, not upload-artifact/download-artifact/cache by name — extending to those three is an application of the same documented, unrestricted mechanism rather than a verbatim quote naming them. Also relevant architecturally (not to this capability claim, but to the A1-vs-A2 decision): a composite action's steps execute within the SAME job as the caller — it cannot itself declare a `strategy: matrix`, so for a matrixed ~4h build, the composite would have to be invoked once per matrix leg from a job that already has the matrix, whereas a reusable workflow (workflow_call) can own the `strategy: matrix` itself. Composite actions also cannot set job-level `needs`/`outputs`-based gating the way a called reusable workflow's job can, and secrets/inputs must be passed explicitly with `with:`/`secrets:` in both models. SHA-pinning is supported for `uses:` inside composite steps (as shown by the `actions/checkout@8f4b7f8...` example), consistent with the repo's pin-by-SHA policy.
votes.implication: This capability alone does not disqualify A1 (composite action): a composite action at .github/actions/kernel-leg/ can freely contain SHA-pinned `uses:` steps for actions/cache, actions/upload-artifact and actions/download-artifact, so artifact handling is not a blocker for either option. The deciding factors are architectural, not capability-based: since the duplicated unit is a matrixed job, A2 (reusable workflow with workflow_call) can own `strategy: matrix` and be listed directly as/within the branch-protection required check with its own job-level `needs` gating, while A1 would require the *caller* job to carry the matrix and invoke the composite action per-leg, then a separate aggregation job (as today) to gate on all matrix legs — functionally workable but pushes the matrix/orchestration logic back into the calling workflow rather than encapsulating it. If the goal is to encapsulate 'the whole matrixed 4h build+upload' as one reusable, independently-callable unit, A2 is the better structural fit; A1 is better if the intent is just to de-duplicate the *step sequence* while keeping matrix/job orchestration in the caller.

votes.evidence: The core mechanism is real and has been GA since Aug 25, 2021 ("GitHub Actions: Reduce duplication with action composition", https://github.blog/changelog/2021-08-25-github-actions-reduce-duplication-with-action-composition/) — composite `runs: using: composite` steps can freely mix `run:` and `uses:`, and community examples confirm `actions/cache@vN` and `actions/upload-artifact`/`download-artifact` work fine as nested `uses:` steps with dynamic `with:` params (e.g. `with: path: ${{ inputs.cache-path }}`, `key: ${{ hashFiles(...) }}`). However I found real, still-standing conditions that break a naive version of the claim: (1) actions/runner#1479 (closed, behavior-as-designed, not a bug fix pending) — the `uses:` value ITSELF must be a static string (owner/repo@ref, SHA, or local path); it cannot be built from `inputs.*` or `matrix.*` expressions, only `with:` params can be dynamic. (2) A `uses:` step's outputs (e.g. `cache-hit` from actions/cache) are NOT automatically surfaced to the calling job — the composite action's action.yml must explicitly declare `outputs:` mapping to `steps.<id>.outputs.cache-hit`, or the caller can't see it. (3) actions/runner#2009 — step-`outputs` context is lost specifically when composite actions are nested inside OTHER local composite actions (two levels deep), a real gotcha if the kernel-leg action itself later wraps another local composite. (4) A widely-cited but distinct false trail: actions/setup-python#377, actions/setup-go#467, actions/setup-node#329 report "caching doesn't work in composite actions" — verified via direct fetch of #377 that this is specifically about setup-python's *built-in* `cache:` input mis-resolving `cache-dependency-path` against `github.action_path` (the `_actions` checkout dir) rather than the caller's workspace; it does NOT indicate that a raw `uses: actions/cache@SHA` step inside a composite is broken, but it pollutes search results and could mislead a team doing due diligence. (5) Composite actions cannot themselves declare a job-level `strategy: matrix` (actions/runner#810, open feature request) — the matrix must live in the calling workflow's job, with the composite invoked once per leg.
votes.implication: For the kernel-leg refactor: A1 (composite action) is not blocked by any version gate — embedding `uses: actions/cache@<sha>`, `uses: actions/upload-artifact@<sha>`, `uses: actions/download-artifact@<sha>` inside .github/actions/kernel-leg/action.yml will work, and since this repo already pins every action by SHA (a static `uses:` value), the one real hard constraint found (uses: cannot be templated by inputs/matrix) is a non-issue — you were never going to template the ref anyway. Two things to design around, not blockers: (a) keep the matrix strategy on the *calling* job in the workflow (`jobs.kernel-leg: strategy: matrix: ...` with `steps: - uses: ./.github/actions/kernel-leg` inside it) since composite actions can't host their own matrix; (b) explicitly wire any values the downstream aggregation/build job needs (e.g. cache-hit status, artifact name) through the composite action's own `outputs:` block mapped to the inner step's `steps.<id>.outputs.*` — nothing is auto-propagated. Neither caveat favors A2 (reusable workflow) over A1 on this specific claim; workflow_call reusable workflows have the analogous matrix-at-call-site requirement and their own explicit `outputs:` wiring needs, so this axis is roughly a wash between A1 and A2 — the deciding factors should be the branch-protection required-check semantics (a reusable workflow's called jobs surface as separate check names unless aggregated) and whether you need `secrets: inherit`-style propagation, not this artifact/cache/uses claim.

votes.evidence:
The literal claim is true and is standard practice: composite actions (`runs: using: composite`) routinely contain `uses:` steps calling actions/checkout, actions/cache, actions/upload-artifact, actions/download-artifact, etc. — this is how thousands of shared setup/build composite actions are written in production repos. `actions/cache`/`upload-artifact`/`download-artifact` authenticate via the runner-injected ACTIONS_RUNTIME_TOKEN/results-service credentials, not the `secrets` context, so composite actions' well-known restriction (no implicit `secrets` context, secrets must be passed as explicit inputs) does NOT block these three actions specifically — confirmed by widespread usage and by GitHub's own composite-action docs describing `uses` steps as fully supported.
However, the 'real practice' lens surfaces real breakage adjacent to this: (1) actions/runner#1271 — composite-action steps do not reliably respect `if: failure()` after a prior step fails; actions/toolkit#1034 shows related `continue-on-error`/`failure()` interaction bugs; actions/runner#2009 shows step outputs aren't available across nested composite actions. These mean the common "upload build logs/partial artifacts even when the build step fails" pattern — very relevant to a 4h kernel build — is unreliable inside a composite action, whereas it is a solid, well-tested pattern for ordinary job steps (which is what a reusable-workflow job's steps are). (2) Composite actions cannot declare `strategy: matrix`, `runs-on`, `permissions`, `timeout-minutes`, or `environment` — those stay on the calling job and must be re-declared in every workflow that calls the composite. (3) Reusable workflows have supported being invoked with a caller-side `strategy: matrix` since Aug 2022, and can also declare their own internal job matrix, contradicting older (~2021) blog posts claiming reusable workflows can't do matrix.
votes.implication: For the kernel-leg sharing decision: the claim being true means A1 (composite action) is technically capable of wrapping the cache/upload-artifact/download-artifact steps for a single matrix leg — it will "work" in the happy path. But it does not carry the parts of the job that actually make this a *duplicated job*, not just duplicated steps: the matrix strategy itself, `runs-on`, `permissions`, and `timeout-minutes` for a 4h build would still have to be copy-pasted into every calling workflow's job definition, which defeats much of the DRY goal. A2 (reusable workflow with `workflow_call`) can own the entire job — matrix, runs-on, permissions, timeout — as one unit, has been matrix-callable since 2022, and each reusable-workflow job appears as its own normal check (so the `status` aggregation job's `needs:` list and branch-protection required-check wiring work exactly as with any other job, no extra indirection). A2 also avoids the documented `if: failure()/always()` propagation bugs (runner#1271, toolkit#1034) that specifically bite long builds wanting failure-path log/artifact uploads. Recommendation: use a reusable workflow (A2) for the shared matrixed kernel-build job itself; composite actions remain the right tool only for smaller, step-level reuse (e.g., a shared "checkout + restore toolchain cache" prelude) nested inside that job, not for the job-with-matrix as a whole. SHA-pinning is unaffected either way — both composite actions and reusable workflows are pinned by SHA identically.

claim: actionlint automatically runs shellcheck against the shell in `run:` blocks, so adding actionlint to CI also lints embedded shell.

votes.lens: Verify strictly against official GitHub Actions documentation and changelogs
votes.evidence: Two things have to be kept separate. (1) docs.github.com (the actual "official GitHub Actions documentation") is silent on actionlint entirely — a site-scoped search returns no hits, because actionlint (rhysd/actionlint) is a third-party static checker, not a GitHub product. So strictly by the "official GitHub docs" lens, there is no official confirmation of this claim. (2) actionlint's own docs (the only authoritative source that actually describes this behavior) confirm the mechanism, with conditions the claim glosses over. From docs/checks.md: "By default, actionlint checks if `shellcheck` command exists in your system and uses it when it is found," and "Only when the shell is `bash` or `sh`, actionlint applies shellcheck to scripts." From docs/usage.md: "If you want to enable [shellcheck integration], install `shellcheck` command. Note that shellcheck is pre-installed on Ubuntu worker" — i.e. the integration is opt-out-able (`-shellcheck=` empty string disables it) and depends on a shellcheck binary being present in whatever environment runs actionlint; it isn't bundled into actionlint itself. It also only covers inline `run:` steps whose resolved shell is bash/sh — not pwsh/cmd steps, and not shell code that lives in separately-invoked script files rather than inline `run:` blocks.
votes.implication: Largely orthogonal to the A1 (composite action) vs A2 (reusable workflow with workflow_call) decision for the kernel-build job — this claim is about linting coverage, not about composability/artifact-passing semantics, which is what should actually drive that choice. One second-order note: if actionlint is added as a CI gate over whichever of .github/actions/kernel-leg/action.yml (A1) or the workflow_call callee YAML (A2) is written, the shellcheck linting "for free" only fires if (a) the runner actionlint executes on has a `shellcheck` binary on PATH — true by default on GitHub-hosted ubuntu-* runners, not guaranteed on self-hosted/containerized runners — and (b) the shell steps in that file use bash/sh (which is the default for both composite-action `run:` steps and workflow `run:` steps on Linux runners, so this repo's kernel-build shell is very likely covered either way). Since both A1 and A2 files consist of ordinary `run:` steps on Linux runners, actionlint+shellcheck coverage is equivalent under either architecture — this claim doesn't favor one over the other.

votes.evidence: Confirmed via actionlint docs (github.com/rhysd/actionlint/blob/main/docs/checks.md, README.md) and GitHub issues/PRs: (1) shellcheck is NOT bundled with actionlint — actionlint only shells out to a `shellcheck` binary if one is found on PATH (or via -shellcheck=/path); if absent, the shellcheck-backed checks are silently skipped and actionlint still exits clean (see issue #477, "pre-commit hook does not install shellcheck, resulting in inconsistent linter behavior"). GitHub-hosted runners happen to ship shellcheck preinstalled, so CI usage usually works, but this is incidental, not guaranteed by actionlint itself. (2) Shellcheck is only invoked for steps whose shell is bash or sh (default on Linux/macOS runners); pwsh/python/etc. run: blocks are never shellchecked, and when the shell can't be statically determined actionlint guesses bash, which can misfire. (3) Several shellcheck codes are hard-disabled in the integration (SC1091, SC2050, SC2194, SC2154, SC2157, SC2043) because of `${{ }}` interpolation, so it's not full shellcheck coverage even when it runs. (4) Most importantly for this decision: actionlint's scope is `.github/workflows/*.yml` (and, for reusable-workflow call sites, the calling workflow) — it does NOT parse or lint standalone composite-action definition files (action.yml/action.yaml) at all. Feature request "feat: Lint actions" (rhysd/actionlint#366) is still open/unmerged as of Feb 2026 (verified live via `gh api repos/rhysd/actionlint/pulls/366` -> state: open, merged: false). A separate community tool (bettermarks/composite-action-lint) exists specifically because actionlint doesn't cover this case.
votes.implication: The claim only holds for run: blocks that live in an actual .github/workflows/*.yml file — which includes a reusable workflow (A2), since workflow_call workflows are themselves files under .github/workflows/. It does NOT hold for a composite action's action.yml (A1): actionlint currently has zero visibility into .github/actions/kernel-leg/action.yml's runs.steps, so the ~4h kernel-build shell embedded there would get no shellcheck linting from actionlint at all (open upstream issue #366, unmerged) unless the repo adds a separate tool (e.g. composite-action-lint) or manually extracts/lints the scripts. If "actionlint catches shell bugs in the extracted job" is a decision factor, that argues for A2 (reusable workflow) over A1 (composite action) — or, if A1 is chosen anyway (e.g. for its native matrix/secrets ergonomics), the repo must not rely on its existing actionlint step for coverage and should add composite-action-lint or move complex shell into a checked-in script that some other linter/shellcheck-direct step still covers. Also note the general caveats (shellcheck must be present on PATH, only bash/sh steps are checked, six SC codes are suppressed) apply regardless of which path is chosen, so "add actionlint" should not be treated as a substitute for an explicit shellcheck step if strict shell-quality gating is a hard requirement for the branch-protection check.

votes.evidence: actionlint's shellcheck integration is real and well-documented (docs/checks.md: "actionlint runs shellcheck for scripts at run: step... only when the shell is bash or sh"), but it has three practical gaps that matter here. (1) It requires the `shellcheck` binary to be present separately on the runner/host — if missing, the check is silently skipped rather than hard-failing, so "added actionlint" can quietly mean "added zero shell linting" unless you use the bundled rhysd/actionlint Docker action or explicitly install shellcheck. (2) actionlint does NOT lint composite action files (`.github/actions/*/action.yml`) — confirmed by two long-standing, still-OPEN upstream feature requests: rhysd/actionlint#46 ("Support linting action metadata files," opened 2021-09-02) and rhysd/actionlint#401 ("Composite actions support?", opened 2024-02-22, 4 comments, no resolution). Its default no-argument invocation only discovers `.github/workflows/*.{yml,yaml}`. (3) Even where it does apply, it suppresses 6 shellcheck codes (SC1091, SC2050, SC2194, SC2154, SC2157, SC2043) to dodge `${{ }}`-masking false positives, so coverage is intentionally reduced-fidelity — and there's an open, unresolved bug (rhysd/actionlint#702) where the shellcheck sub-process can deadlock when a `run:` script's stdin exceeds kernel pipe capacity, i.e. it can hang CI on large embedded scripts rather than just report findings.
votes.implication: This asymmetry should tip the decision toward A2 (reusable workflow via workflow_call) over A1 (composite action) if "adding actionlint" is meant to actually cover the shared kernel-build job's shell. A reusable workflow is just another file under .github/workflows/*.yml, so it falls under actionlint's normal auto-discovery and gets full shellcheck treatment on every `run:` block in the 4h matrixed build — consistent with whatever the repo's existing `status` required-check job already does for other workflow files.
